title: Antimycobacterial Agents
authors: Divya Reddy, Sebastian G. Kurz, Max R. O'Donnell

items.title: Optimal Regimen for Latent TB Infection
items.content: The preferred regimen for treating latent TB infection (LTBI) in children over 2 years and adults, including HIV-positive individuals, is a 3-month weekly course of isoniazid plus rifapentine. This regimen is favored due to its effectiveness, safety, tolerability, and noninferior completion rates compared to directly observed therapy.
keyPoints: Isoniazid plus rifapentine for 3 months (weekly) is the regimen of choice for LTBI in children >2 years and adults including HIV-positive individuals.
Shorter duration rifamycin-based regimens are preferred over isoniazid for 6–9 months due to effectiveness, safety, and tolerability.
Regimen not recommended for pregnant women or those with hypersensitivity reactions to isoniazid or rifampin.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Duration and Regimens for Drug-Susceptible TB
items.content: For drug-susceptible tuberculosis, the standard treatment involves an intensive phase of four drugs—isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol—for 2 months, followed by a continuation phase with isoniazid and rifampin for 4 months. The total duration can extend to 9 months in cases of cavitary disease or delayed culture conversion.
keyPoints: Standard regimen: Intensive phase (H, R, Z, E) for 2 months followed by continuation phase (H, R) for 4 months.
Total treatment duration is typically 6 months but can extend to 9 months for cavitary disease or if sputum cultures remain positive beyond 2 months.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.title: New Regimen for Drug-Susceptible TB
items.content: A new 4-month regimen involving daily rifapentine, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and moxifloxacin for 8 weeks, followed by rifapentine, isoniazid, and moxifloxacin for 9 weeks, has been shown to be noninferior to the traditional 6-month HRZE regimen. This regimen includes patients with HIV and a CD4 count >100.
keyPoints: 4-month regimen: Daily rifapentine, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, moxifloxacin for 8 weeks; then rifapentine, isoniazid, moxifloxacin for 9 weeks.
Noninferior to traditional 6-month HRZE regimen.
Includes patients with HIV and CD4 count >100.
Concerns include higher daily pill burden and potential side effects from prolonged use of moxifloxacin.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.title: Timing and Monitoring for ART in HIV/TB Co-infection
items.content: Initiation timing of antiretroviral therapy (ART) in HIV patients co-infected with M. tuberculosis is crucial to improve survival, dependent on CD4+ T-cell count.
keyPoints: Start ART within 2 weeks if CD4+ T-cell count ≤ 50/μL
Begin ART by 8–12 weeks if CD4+ T-cell count ≥ 50/μL
Monitor for drug interactions, especially rifampin with protease inhibitors or nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors
Close monitoring and dose adjustments are necessary due to potential significant interactions

items.title: Management of TB IRIS in HIV/TB Co-infected Patients
items.content: TB immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) can occur after ART initiation, requiring careful management to prevent severe complications.
keyPoints: TB IRIS may appear as early as 1 week post-ART initiation
Manifests as paradoxical worsening or unmasking of TB infection
Conservative management involves continued ART and TB medications
Severe IRIS can be treated with glucocorticoids, such as a 4-week course of prednisone to reduce hospitalization and improve symptoms

items.title: Approach to Treatment Failure in HIV/TB Co-infected Patients
items.content: If clinical improvement is absent or the patient's condition worsens, consider treatment failure due to adherence issues, absorption problems, resistance development, or IRIS.
keyPoints: Consider incomplete adherence, poor medication absorption, or resistance if no significant improvement occurs
IRIS should be considered as a diagnosis of exclusion in deteriorating patients
Repeat drug susceptibility testing if resistance is suspected
Add at least two new efficacious drugs to the regimen if resistance is confirmed

items.title: Impact of Novel Drugs on MDR-TB Treatment Duration
items.content: The introduction of novel drugs like bedaquiline and pretomanid has enabled the development of shortened all-oral regimens for multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB). The Nix-TB study demonstrated that a 6-month regimen consisting of bedaquiline, pretomanid, and linezolid achieved favorable outcomes in 89% of patients with highly drug-resistant TB.
keyPoints: The Bangladesh regimen reduced treatment duration to 9–12 months with favorable outcomes in up to 90% of patients.
A meta-analysis showed better outcomes with linezolid, bedaquiline, clofazimine, carbapenems, and later generation fluoroquinolones; worse outcomes with kanamycin and capreomycin.
The Nix-TB study reported favorable outcomes in 89% of patients using a 6-month regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid) for highly drug-resistant TB.

items.title: Efficacy and Safety of the BPaLM Regimen
items.content: The TB PRACTECAL trial evaluated a 24-week regimen of bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid, and moxifloxacin (BPaLM) against a standard-care regimen for rifampicin-resistant TB. The study found the BPaLM regimen to be noninferior in efficacy with fewer serious adverse events compared to the standard-care regimen.
keyPoints: The TB PRACTECAL trial was an open-label, multicenter, randomized, controlled, noninferiority trial.
BPaLM regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid, moxifloxacin) showed noninferior efficacy to standard-care regimens in both intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses.
The BPaLM regimen had a lower percentage of serious adverse events compared to the standard-care regimen.

items.title: Isoniazid Regimens and Efficacy in TB Treatment
items.content: Isoniazid is a cornerstone drug for treating both active tuberculosis (TB) disease and latent TB infection (LTBI). It has excellent bactericidal activity against actively dividing M. tuberculosis, while being bacteriostatic against slowly dividing organisms. For LTBI, various regimens are effective: daily dosing for 6 months or intermittent twice-weekly dosing under directly observed therapy (DOT) for the same duration. A weekly regimen of isoniazid with rifapentine over 3 months has shown noninferiority to a traditional 9-month daily isoniazid regimen, with higher completion rates.
keyPoints: Isoniazid is critical for TB and LTBI treatment due to its bactericidal activity against actively dividing M. tuberculosis.
For LTBI: Daily dosing for 6 months or twice-weekly under DOT for 6 months are equivalent; a weekly regimen with rifapentine over 3 months has higher completion rates.
A novel 1-month regimen combining isoniazid and rifapentine is expected in new WHO guidelines.

items.title: Mechanism of Action of Isoniazid
items.content: Isoniazid acts as a prodrug, activated by the mycobacterial enzyme KatG catalase-peroxidase. This activation leads to the formation of an isonicotinic acyl-NADH complex that inhibits InhA, a key enzyme in fatty acid synthase, thereby blocking mycolic acid synthesis essential for mycobacterial cell wall integrity. Additionally, free radicals released during this process contribute to its antimycobacterial activity.
keyPoints: Isoniazid is activated by KatG catalase-peroxidase to form an acyl-NADH complex that inhibits InhA, blocking fatty acid synthase and mycolic acid synthesis.
Mycolic acids are crucial for the mycobacterial cell wall structure.
Free radicals released during activation have additional antimycobacterial effects.

items.title: Pharmacology and Dosing of Isoniazid
items.content: Isoniazid is a small, water-soluble molecule that achieves peak serum levels sufficient to exceed the minimal inhibitory concentrations for susceptible M. tuberculosis strains. It diffuses well throughout the body, including into cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). Metabolized in the liver by acetylation and hydrolysis, genetic variations in N-acetyltransferase 2 (NAT2) affect isoniazid levels and toxicity risk. The recommended daily dose for TB treatment is 5 mg/kg for adults, with a maximum of 300 mg, while intermittent therapy doses are higher.
keyPoints: Isoniazid achieves peak serum levels of 3–5 μg/mL within 30 min to 2 h post-ingestion, exceeding MICs for susceptible M. tuberculosis strains (<0.1 μg/mL).
Diffuses well throughout the body, including CSF; no dosage adjustment needed in renal disease.
Dosing: 5 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) for TB treatment; intermittent therapy dosing is higher at 15 mg/kg.

items.title: Understanding Isoniazid Resistance Mechanisms
items.content: Isoniazid resistance in M. tuberculosis is primarily due to genetic mutations and increased efflux pump gene expression.
keyPoints: Approximately 7% of clinical M. tuberculosis isolates in the United States are resistant to isoniazid.

Resistance pathways include amino acid changes in katG or inhA, alterations in kasA, loss of NADH dehydrogenase 2 activity, and increased expression of efflux pump genes (efpA, mmpL7, mmr, p55, Rv1258c).
Increased efflux pump gene expression is implicated in 20–30% of resistant isolates.

items.title: Managing Isoniazid-Induced Adverse Effects
items.content: Isoniazid can cause liver injury and peripheral neuropathy, necessitating careful monitoring in high-risk patients.
keyPoints: Adverse effects include asymptomatic aminotransferase elevation (up to 20%), rash (2%), fever (1.2%), symptomatic hepatitis (<0.1%), fulminant hepatitis (<0.01%).
Peripheral neuropathy occurs in up to 2% of patients at doses ≥5 mg/kg.
Prophylactic pyridoxine is recommended for high-risk patients, including those with HIV, diabetes, alcohol abuse, or malnutrition.

items.title: Rifampin's Role and Pharmacology in TB Treatment
items.content: Rifampin is a cornerstone of TB treatment due to its bactericidal activity, but it requires monitoring for drug interactions.
keyPoints: Rifampin inhibits mycobacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, blocking RNA synthesis at concentrations of 1 µg/mL.
It achieves serum levels of 10–20 µg/mL within 2.5 hours post-administration and has a half-life of 1.5–5 hours.
Rifampin induces the hepatic cytochrome P450 system, affecting drugs like warfarin, phenytoin, and oral contraceptives.

items.title: Rifampin Dosing and Resistance
items.content: Rifampin is dosed at 10 mg/kg for adults, with a maximum of 600 mg/d. It does not require dose adjustments in renal insufficiency. Resistance arises from mutations in the rpoB gene affecting RNA polymerase.
keyPoints: Dose: 10 mg/kg for adults; max 600 mg/d
No adjustment needed for renal insufficiency
Resistance due to missense point mutations in rpoB gene

items.title: Pyrazinamide's Role and Mechanism
items.content: Pyrazinamide is crucial for shortening TB treatment duration by being active against slowly replicating M. tuberculosis in acidic environments, targeting fatty acid synthetase I.
keyPoints: Shortens treatment from 9 to 6 months
Active at pH <6.0, targets fatty acid synthetase I
Effective pyrazinamide concentration: 16–50 µg/mL at pH 5.5

items.title: Ethambutol's Use and Efficacy
items.content: Ethambutol is a bacteriostatic agent used in the first-line regimen, providing synergy with other drugs. It is effective against M. tuberculosis and MAC but less potent than other first-line agents.
keyPoints: Bacteriostatic agent synthesized in 1961
Effective against M. tuberculosis, M. marinum, M. kansasii, and MAC
Least potent among first-line drugs for M. tuberculosis

items.title: Ethambutol's Mechanism and Resistance
items.content: Ethambutol acts as a bacteriostatic agent against M. tuberculosis by inhibiting arabinosyltransferases, crucial for cell wall synthesis. Resistance is primarily due to missense mutations in the embB gene, particularly at codon 306.
keyPoints: Mechanism: Inhibition of arabinosyltransferases involved in cell wall synthesis.
MIC for susceptible strains: 0.5–2 µg/mL.
Resistance: Associated with missense mutations in the embB gene, especially at codon 306 (50–70% of cases).

items.title: Ethambutol Dosing and Ocular Toxicity
items.content: Ethambutol is absorbed quickly, with peak serum levels at 2–4 µg/mL after a standard dose. It can cause optic neuritis, necessitating baseline visual tests and regular monitoring. Dosage adjustments are needed for renal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Absorption: 75–80% within 2–4 hours; peak serum levels at 2–4 µg/mL after a standard dose of 15 mg/kg.
Ocular Toxicity: Risk is dose-dependent (1–5%), with symptoms like reduced visual acuity and central scotoma.
Dosing Adjustments: Required for renal insufficiency; baseline visual tests recommended.

items.title: Rifabutin as an Alternative in HIV-Co-Infected TB Patients on Protease Inhibitors
items.content: Rifabutin is preferred over rifampin for treating tuberculosis (TB) in patients co-infected with HIV who are taking protease inhibitors or nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, especially nevirapine. This preference is due to its less pronounced effect on hepatic enzyme induction and better treatment outcomes observed in studies.
keyPoints: Rifabutin inhibits mycobacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase.
Recommended over rifampin for HIV-TB co-infected patients on protease inhibitors or nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors like nevirapine.
Study in India showed better TB outcomes with daily rifabutin plus atazanavir/ritonavir compared to thrice-weekly regimen.
Rifabutin's hepatic enzyme induction is less pronounced than rifampin, reducing drug interactions.

items.title: Pharmacokinetics and Adverse Effects of Rifabutin
items.content: Rifabutin is rapidly absorbed after oral administration, with peak serum levels reached 2–4 hours post-ingestion. It has a prolonged half-life of 45 hours due to slow clearance by the hepatic microsomal system. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal symptoms and rash, while serious reactions can involve liver enzyme elevation and hematologic abnormalities.
keyPoints: Rifabutin is lipophilic with peak serum levels reached in 2–4 hours.
Mean serum half-life of rifabutin is 45 hours due to slow clearance.
Common adverse effects: gastrointestinal symptoms, rash, headache, asthenia, chest pain, myalgia, insomnia.
Serious reactions include liver enzyme elevation, neutropenia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia.

items.title: Rifapentine Use in TB Treatment and Resistance Considerations
items.content: Rifapentine is used as an alternative to rifampin during the continuation phase of treatment for noncavitary drug-susceptible pulmonary TB in HIV-seronegative patients with negative sputum smears. It allows for less frequent dosing due to its prolonged half-life. Resistance to rifapentine, like rifampin, is mediated by mutations in rpoB.
keyPoints: Rifapentine shares a mechanism of action with rifampin and has a prolonged half-life (~13 hours).
Used once weekly with isoniazid for noncavitary drug-susceptible pulmonary TB in HIV-seronegative patients with negative sputum smears.
Not recommended for TB-HIV co-infected patients or children <12 years due to higher relapse rates and lack of approval.
Resistance mediated by mutations in rpoB, similar to rifampin.

items.title: Fluoroquinolones in TB Treatment: Potential for Shortened Regimen
items.content: Fluoroquinolones, by inhibiting mycobacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, have shown potential to shorten the treatment duration of drug-susceptible TB from 6 months to 4 months. This is demonstrated in a recent trial (TBTC Study 31) where a regimen including rifapentine and moxifloxacin was noninferior to the standard regimen.
keyPoints: Fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, preventing cell replication and protein synthesis.
Recent TBTC Study 31 showed that a 4-month regimen with rifapentine and moxifloxacin was noninferior to the standard 6-month regimen for drug-susceptible TB.
Current recommendations still favor a standard 6-month regimen, but future guidelines may change based on these results.

items.title: Safety and Efficacy of Fluoroquinolones in MDR-TB
items.content: Levofloxacin and moxifloxacin are effective for treating multi-drug resistant TB (MDR-TB). High-dose moxifloxacin (800 mg) is recommended for standardized shorter regimens. Despite concerns about QTc interval prolongation, fluoroquinolones are considered safe alternatives when first-line agents cause adverse effects.
keyPoints: Levofloxacin and moxifloxacin are used effectively in MDR-TB treatment.
High-dose moxifloxacin (800 mg) is recommended for shorter MDR-TB regimens.
Fluoroquinolones are safe alternatives for patients with adverse effects from first-line agents.

items.title: Considerations in Fluoroquinolone Use: Resistance and Drug Interactions
items.content: Empirical fluoroquinolone therapy can delay TB diagnosis and increase resistance. Mutations in gyrA and gyrB genes are common causes of clinical resistance. Bedaquiline, a diarylquinoline, faces resistance due to mutations in the atpE gene and other non-target sites.
keyPoints: Empirical fluoroquinolone therapy can delay TB diagnosis and increase resistance.
Mutations in gyrA and gyrB genes are implicated in fluoroquinolone resistance.
Bedaquiline resistance is associated with mutations in the atpE gene and other non-target sites.

items.title: Bedaquiline for MDR-TB: Efficacy and Safety
items.content: Bedaquiline, with excellent oral bioavailability and a long elimination half-life (>14 days), is used in treating multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB). It improves 2-month sputum culture-conversion rates when added to standard regimens. However, it carries a 'black box' warning due to higher mortality observed in one trial (11.4% vs 2.5%) and potential QT prolongation.
keyPoints: Oral bioavailability of bedaquiline is excellent.
Dosage: 400 mg/day for the first 2 weeks, then 200 mg thrice weekly for a total of 6 months.
Elimination half-life >14 days.
Improves 2-month sputum culture-conversion rate in MDR-TB.
'Black box' warning due to higher mortality (11.4% vs 2.5%) and QT prolongation.

items.title: Linezolid in Drug-Resistant TB
items.content: Linezolid, an oxazolidinone, is effective against drug-resistant TB due to its mechanism of disrupting protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosome. It has nearly 100% oral bioavailability and good tissue penetration. While ~80% of MDR-TB patients can be treated successfully with linezolid-containing regimens, it may cause optic/peripheral neuropathy, pancytopenia, lactic acidosis, and serotonin syndrome.
keyPoints: Linezolid disrupts protein synthesis by binding to the 50S ribosome.
Nearly 100% oral bioavailability with good tissue penetration.
~80% of MDR-TB patients can be treated successfully with linezolid-containing regimens.
Adverse effects: optic/peripheral neuropathy, pancytopenia, lactic acidosis, serotonin syndrome.

items.title: Clofazimine's Role in MDR-TB Treatment
items.content: Clofazimine is used for drug-resistant TB due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. It has a long half-life (~70 days) and can improve treatment outcomes when included in multidrug regimens. Common side effects include gastrointestinal intolerance and skin discoloration.
keyPoints: Half-life of ~70 days, steady-state concentrations achieved at ~1 month.
Used for its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties in MDR-TB.
Improves treatment outcomes when included in multidrug regimens.
Side effects: gastrointestinal intolerance, skin discoloration.

items.title: Cycloserine: Mechanism and Caution
items.content: Cycloserine, an analogue of d-alanine, inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by targeting enzymes like alanine racemase. It is effective against M. tuberculosis but has significant side effects including seizures and psychosis.
keyPoints: Inhibits enzymes involved in peptidoglycan production
Usual adult dosage: 250 mg two or three times per day
Side effects include seizures, psychosis, peripheral neuropathy, headache, somnolence, allergic reactions
Monitor drug levels to reduce adverse effects, especially in renal failure patients
Administer with caution for patients with epilepsy, active alcohol abuse, severe renal insufficiency, or history of depression/psychosis

items.title: Delamanid: Efficacy and Safety
items.content: Delamanid is a nitroimidazole derivative that inhibits mycolic acid biosynthesis. It improves culture conversion rates but has been associated with QT prolongation.
keyPoints: Activates via M. tuberculosis-specific flavin-dependent nitroreductases
Improves 2-month culture conversion rate in clinical trials
QT prolongation more common, no clinically relevant events reported
No significant difference in 6-month sputum conversion with optimized regimen
Recommended for children <6 years with rifampicin-resistant TB
Usual adult dose: 100 mg twice daily

items.title: Pretomanid: Promising Results and Concerns
items.content: Pretomanid, combined with bedaquiline or other agents, shows promise in treating drug-resistant TB but raises concerns for hepatotoxicity.
keyPoints: Combination with bedaquiline effective for drug-resistant TB
Higher culture conversion rates at 8 weeks compared to HRZE
Concerns of potentially fatal hepatotoxicity noted
FDA approval for specific highly resistant TB cases
Usual adult dose: 200 mg daily

items.title: Use of Aminoglycosides in TB Treatment
items.content: Aminoglycosides such as amikacin and streptomycin are used for their mycobactericidal activity against M. tuberculosis by binding to the 16S ribosomal subunit. Despite their efficacy, WHO recommends limiting their use due to serious side effects and availability of novel oral agents.
keyPoints: Amikacin and streptomycin bind to the 16S ribosomal subunit for mycobactericidal activity.
They are effective against M. tuberculosis, several NTM species, and some aerobic gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria.
WHO recommends limiting use due to serious side effects like ototoxicity (up to 10% of recipients), nephrotoxicity, and neurotoxicity.
Kanamycin and capreomycin are not recommended due to worse outcomes and increased mortality.

items.title: Ethionamide in Drug-Resistant TB Treatment
items.content: Ethionamide, a derivative of isonicotinic acid, inhibits mycolic acid synthesis and is used for drug-resistant TB. Its use is limited by severe gastrointestinal reactions and neurologic side effects.
keyPoints: Inhibits the $ inhA $ gene product enoyl-acyl carrier protein (acp) reductase involved in mycolic acid synthesis.
Bacteriostatic against metabolically active M. tuberculosis and some NTM.
Adverse effects include severe gastrointestinal reactions, central and peripheral neurologic side effects, reversible hepatitis (~5% of recipients), hypersensitivity reactions, and hypothyroidism.
Should be taken with food and pyridoxine (50–100 mg/d) to reduce side effects.

items.title: Distinguishing NTM from M. tuberculosis based on growth characteristics
items.content: Nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) can be differentiated from Mycobacterium tuberculosis by their growth rates in culture. While M. tuberculosis typically takes longer to grow, NTM species often show growth within 2–3 weeks, which is a critical preliminary diagnostic indicator.
keyPoints: Growth within 1 week indicates rapidly growing NTM species.
Growth within 2–3 weeks suggests an NTM rather than M. tuberculosis.
Distinguishing between colonization and active disease in NTM is crucial for treatment decisions.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.title: Criteria for diagnosing NTM pulmonary disease
items.content: Diagnosis of NTM pulmonary disease requires significant clinical manifestations or radiographic evidence of progressive disease, along with reproducible sputum culture results or a single positive culture from bronchoscopy. Isolation of NTM from blood or extrapulmonary sites indicates disseminated or local infection.
keyPoints: Significant clinical and/or radiographic evidence is required for diagnosis.
Reproducible sputum cultures or a single positive bronchoscopic culture are diagnostic criteria.
Isolation from blood or extrapulmonary sites suggests disseminated or localized NTM infection.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.title: Optimal Regimen for MAC Lung Disease
items.content: For nodular or bronchiectatic MAC lung disease, the initial treatment consists of clarithromycin or azithromycin, rifampin or rifabutin, and ethambutol administered three times per week. In severe cases like fibrocavitary macular disease, a daily regimen including amikacin or streptomycin is recommended.
keyPoints: Initial treatment for nodular/bronchiectatic MAC: clarithromycin or azithromycin, rifampin or rifabutin (rifabutin preferred for HIV patients on ART), ethambutol, three times per week.
Daily regimen recommended for fibrocavitary macular disease with consideration of amikacin/streptomycin initially.
Treatment duration: at least 12 months after culture conversion.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.title: Use of Inhaled Liposomal Amikacin in Refractory MAC
items.content: In the CONVERT trial, adding inhaled liposomal amikacin to a standard three-drug regimen for treatment-refractory MAC lung disease increased culture conversion rates from 9% to 26% at 6 months. Despite higher respiratory adverse events (87.4%), serious adverse event rates were similar between groups.
keyPoints: Inhaled liposomal amikacin increases culture conversion in refractory MAC from 9% to 26% at 6 months.
Respiratory adverse events: 87.4% with inhaled liposomal amikacin vs. 50% with standard therapy.
Serious adverse event rates were not different between regimens.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.title: Management of MAC in HIV-Infected Patients
items.content: For HIV-infected patients with well-controlled disease, MAC treatment mirrors that for non-HIV patients, but drug-drug interactions between antimycobacterial agents and ART must be considered. In those with CD4+ T-cell counts <100/μL, disseminated MAC is treated with clarithromycin, ethambutol, rifabutin, potentially replacing clarithromycin with azithromycin based on tolerance.
keyPoints: MAC treatment in well-controlled HIV patients follows non-HIV protocols; consider drug-drug interactions with ART.
Disseminated MAC in low CD4 count (<100/μL) treated with clarithromycin, ethambutol, rifabutin.
Azithromycin may replace clarithromycin based on adverse effects and tolerance.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.title: Management of M. abscessus Infections
items.content: M. abscessus, a rapidly growing mycobacterium and the third most common NTM pathogen in the U.S., requires complex treatment due to its resistance to standard anti-TB regimens. Treatment involves macrolides (clarithromycin or azithromycin) combined with parenteral agents like amikacin for skin and soft tissue infections, while bone infections require at least 6 months of therapy. Lung infections are challenging due to drug toxicities and often necessitate a regimen of three active drugs based on in vitro susceptibility testing.
keyPoints: M. abscessus is the third most common NTM pathogen in the U.S., particularly endemic in southeastern states.
Skin, soft tissue, and bone infections require macrolide therapy combined with parenteral agents for a minimum of 4 months; bone infections need at least 6 months.
Lung infection treatment involves three active drugs (amikacin, linezolid, tigecycline, imipenem, azithromycin if susceptible) based on susceptibility testing.
Therapeutic efficiency in M. abscessus lung infection is ~35%; amikacin, imipenem, linezolid, and/or tigecycline improve outcomes.
Surgical resection should be considered for patients with good lung reserve and localized infections.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.title: Clarithromycin Use in NTM Treatment
items.content: Clarithromycin, a macrolide antibiotic effective against many NTM species including MAC, inhibits protein synthesis by binding to the 50S ribosomal subunit. Resistance is often due to ermB gene overexpression leading to methylation of the binding site. M. abscessus may exhibit inducible macrolide resistance via erm41 after incubation with macrolides for 3–5 days. Clarithromycin dosage varies based on infection type, and it should be used in combination regimens to prevent resistance.
keyPoints: Clarithromycin inhibits protein synthesis by binding to the 50S mycobacterial ribosomal subunit.
Resistance is often due to ermB gene overexpression causing methylation of the binding site.
M. abscessus may have inducible macrolide resistance via erm41, becoming apparent after 3-5 days of incubation with macrolides.
Dosage: 500 mg BID TID for nodular/bronchiectatic MAC; 500–1000 mg daily for fibrocavitary or severe infections; 1000 mg daily for disseminated infection.
Used in combination regimens to avoid resistance, typically with ethambutol and a rifamycin.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.title: Azithromycin as an Alternative to Clarithromycin in NTM Treatment
items.content: Azithromycin, similar to clarithromycin but with better tolerability and fewer drug interactions, is preferred for treating MAC infections due to its once-daily dosing. Resistance to azithromycin implies cross-resistance to clarithromycin. It penetrates tissues well and has a prolonged half-life, making it suitable for combination therapy to prevent resistance.
keyPoints: Azithromycin is preferred over clarithromycin due to once-daily dosing, better tolerability, fewer drug interactions, and equal efficacy.
Resistance to azithromycin implies complete cross-resistance to clarithromycin.
Dosage for MAC infection: 250 mg daily or 500 mg TID; 1200 mg weekly for prophylaxis in immunocompromised individuals.
Azithromycin penetrates tissues well and has a prolonged half-life (~48 h).
Not metabolized by cytochrome P450, thus fewer drug interactions.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.question: Which regimen is currently the treatment of choice for latent TB infection (LTBI) in children >2 years and adults, including HIV-positive individuals?
options: Isoniazid plus rifampin daily for 3 months
Isoniazid plus rifapentine weekly for 3 months
Isoniazid alone optimally daily or twice weekly for 6–9 months
items.explanation: The regimen of isoniazid plus rifapentine weekly for 3 months is the current choice for LTBI in children >2 years and adults, including HIV-positive individuals.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: What is a key reason for caution when treating latent TB infection with rifamycin-based regimens in HIV-positive individuals?
options: High risk of severe side effects
Potential for drug interactions and lack of definitive data on effectiveness
Inability to use these drugs in immunocompromised patients
items.explanation: Caution is advised due to potential for drug interactions, lack of definitive data on effectiveness, and the possibility of subclinical TB disease.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: What was a significant advancement in the treatment of tuberculosis that occurred in 1943?
options: Isolation of streptomycin from Streptomyces griseus
Development of the tuberculin skin test
items.explanation: The isolation of streptomycin in 1943 marked a significant advancement, launching the era of antibiotic treatment for TB.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: Which regimen was shown to be noninferior to the traditional 6-month HRZE regimen in a large multinational randomized trial?
options: 4 months of isoniazid, rifampin, and ethambutol
8 weeks of daily rifapentine, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, moxifloxacin followed by 9 weeks of rifapentine, isoniazid, moxifloxacin
3 months of isoniazid plus rifapentine weekly
items.explanation: The regimen consisting of daily rifapentine, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and moxifloxacin for 8 weeks followed by rifapentine, isoniazid, and moxifloxacin for 9 weeks was noninferior to the traditional 6-month HRZE regimen.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.question: When should antiretroviral therapy (ART) be initiated in HIV patients co-infected with M. tuberculosis if the CD4+ T-cell count is ≤50/μL?
options: Within 8–12 weeks of TB treatment initiation
Within 2 weeks of the initiation of TB treatment
items.explanation: ART should be initiated within 2 weeks of the initiation of TB treatment if CD4+ T-cell count is ≤50/μL.

items.question: Which drug is a less potent cytochrome P3A inhibitor than rifampin and can be used as an alternative in HIV patients co-infected with M. tuberculosis?
items.explanation: Rifabutin is a less potent cytochrome P3A inhibitor than rifampin and can be used as an alternative.

items.question: What therapy has been shown to significantly reduce the need for hospitalization in TB immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)?
items.explanation: A 4-week course of prednisone has been shown to significantly reduce the need for hospitalization in TB IRIS.

items.question: What is the WHO's emphasis in their new 2022 recommendations for treating drug-resistant tuberculosis?
options: Using an all-oral bedaquiline-containing regimen with a goal of limiting treatment duration to 6 months
Increasing doses of first-line TB drugs
Avoiding the use of second-line agents
items.explanation: The WHO's new recommendations emphasize using an all-oral bedaquiline-containing regimen with a goal of limiting treatment duration to 6 months.

items.question: Which regimen was the first short-course MDR-TB regimen systematically studied in the STREAM-1 trial?
items.explanation: The Bangladesh regimen was the first short-course MDR-TB regimen systematically studied in the STREAM-1 trial.

items.question: Which drugs were associated with significantly better treatment outcomes for MDR-TB according to a large meta-analysis in 2018?
options: Linezolid, bedaquiline, clofazimine, carbapenems, later generation fluoroquinolones
items.explanation: The meta-analysis showed better outcomes with linezolid, bedaquiline, clofazimine, carbapenems, and later generation fluoroquinolones.

items.question: What was the primary concern raised regarding the BPaL regimen for highly drug-resistant TB?
options: Inferior efficacy compared to standard-care regimens
items.explanation: Caution was raised regarding the higher rate of side effects, mostly due to linezolid.

items.question: Which of the following is a mechanism by which isoniazid exerts its antimycobacterial activity?
options: Isoniazid inhibits protein synthesis in M. tuberculosis.
Isoniazid blocks mycobacterial ketoenoyl-reductase (InhA) and inhibits fatty acid synthase.
Isoniazid disrupts the DNA replication of M. tuberculosis.
Isoniazid interferes with RNA transcription in M. tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Isoniazid blocks mycobacterial ketoenoyl-reductase (InhA) and inhibits fatty acid synthase, which is essential for mycolic acid synthesis.

items.question: What is the recommended daily dose of isoniazid for adults in TB treatment?
items.explanation: The recommended daily dose of isoniazid for the treatment of TB in adults is 5 mg/kg.

items.question: Which factor affects the serum levels and potential toxicity of isoniazid in patients?
items.explanation: The acetylation phenotype (fast or slow) affects serum levels and potential toxicity of isoniazid.

items.question: What is the maximal daily dose of isoniazid for adults in intermittent therapy?
items.explanation: The maximal daily dose of isoniazid for adults in intermittent therapy (usually twice per week) is 900 mg.

items.question: Which gene alteration is most commonly associated with isoniazid resistance in M. tuberculosis?
items.explanation: Most strains have amino acid changes in either the catalase-peroxidase gene (katG) or the mycobacterial ketoenoylreductase gene (inhA).

items.question: What is a significant adverse effect of isoniazid that occurs in up to 20% of recipients?
options: Asymptomatic transient elevation of aminotransferase levels
items.explanation: Isoniazid may cause asymptomatic transient elevation of aminotransferase levels (often termed hepatic adaptation) in up to 20% of recipients.

items.question: Which factor increases the incidence of isoniazid-associated hepatitis?
items.explanation: The incidence of isoniazid-associated hepatitis increases with age, with daily alcohol consumption.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which rifampin exerts its bactericidal activity?
options: Inhibition of mycobacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase
items.explanation: Rifampin specifically binds to and inhibits mycobacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, blocking RNA synthesis.

items.question: What is the maximum daily dosage of rifampin for both adults and children?
items.explanation: The passage states that the maximum daily dosage of rifampin is 600 mg for both adults and children.

items.question: Which enzyme's activity loss leads to pyrazinamide resistance in M. tuberculosis?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the basis of pyrazinamide resistance is a mutation in the pncA gene coding for pyrazinamidase, which converts pyrazinamide to its active form.

items.question: In what type of environment is pyrazinoic acid (POA) active?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that POA, the active form of pyrazinamide, is active only in acidic environments (pH <6.0).

items.question: Which adverse effect is commonly associated with pyrazinamide but rarely leads to clinical gout?
items.explanation: The passage states that hyperuricemia is a common adverse effect of pyrazinamide therapy, which usually can be managed conservatively and rarely leads to clinical gout.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of action of ethambutol against M. tuberculosis?
options: Inhibition of arabinosyltransferases involved in cell wall synthesis
items.explanation: Ethambutol inhibits the arabinosyltransferases involved in cell wall synthesis.

items.question: Which adverse effect is most serious and commonly associated with ethambutol?
items.explanation: The most serious adverse effect of ethambutol is optic neuritis.

items.question: What factor increases the risk of ocular toxicity from ethambutol?
items.explanation: The risk of ocular toxicity from ethambutol is increased by renal insufficiency.

items.question: Which drug should be used instead of rifampin for the treatment of TB in HIV-co-infected individuals taking protease inhibitors or nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, particularly nevirapine?
items.explanation: Rifabutin is recommended in place of rifampin for TB treatment in HIV-co-infected individuals on protease inhibitors or nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors.

items.question: Which drug's effect on hepatic enzyme induction is less pronounced than that of rifampin?
items.explanation: Rifabutin's effect on hepatic enzyme induction is less pronounced than that of rifampin.

items.question: Which drug has a mean serum half-life of 45 hours, much longer than the 3- to 5-hour half-life of rifampin?
items.explanation: Rifabutin has a mean serum half-life of 45 hours, much longer than rifampin's.

items.question: Which drug is administered once weekly with isoniazid during the continuation phase for noncavitary drug-susceptible pulmonary TB in HIV-seronegative patients?
items.explanation: Rifapentine is administered once weekly with isoniazid during the continuation phase for noncavitary drug-susceptible pulmonary TB in HIV-seronegative patients.

items.question: Which fluoroquinolone is no longer recommended for the treatment of TB due to poor efficacy?
items.explanation: Ciprofloxacin and ofloxacin are no longer recommended for the treatment of TB because of poor efficacy.

items.question: What is a significant adverse effect associated with gatifloxacin that has led to its decreased use?
items.explanation: Gatifloxacin has fallen out of favor because of significant dysglycemia.

items.question: Which drug is metabolized by the hepatic cytochrome CYP3A4 and is affected by rifampin?
items.explanation: Bedaquiline is metabolized by the hepatic cytochrome CYP3A4 and rifampin lowers its levels.

items.question: Which fluoroquinolone has been associated with favorable outcomes in patients with drug-resistant TB despite resistance to earlier generations?
items.explanation: Use of later-generation fluoroquinolones like levofloxacin has been associated with favorable outcomes in drug-resistant TB.

items.question: What is the initial dosage regimen for bedaquiline in treating tuberculosis?
options: 400 mg/d for the first 2 weeks, then 200 mg daily for 6 months
400 mg/d for the first 2 weeks, then 200 mg thrice weekly typically for 6 months total
600 mg twice daily for the first month, followed by 300 mg daily
items.explanation: The passage specifies that bedaquiline is dosed at 400 mg/d for the first 2 weeks and then 200 mg thrice weekly typically for a total of 6 months.

items.question: Which adverse effect is associated with linezolid when used in higher doses?
options: Optic and peripheral neuropathy, pancytopenia, lactic acidosis
Orange to brownish discoloration of skin
items.explanation: The passage states that linezolid's adverse effects at higher doses include optic and peripheral neuropathy, pancytopenia, and lactic acidosis.

items.question: What is a potential side effect of clofazimine?
options: Orange to brownish discoloration of skin, bodily fluids, and secretions
items.explanation: The passage mentions that clofazimine can cause reversible orange to brownish discoloration of the skin, bodily fluids, and secretions.

items.question: What is a characteristic of sutezolid compared to linezolid?
options: Higher early bactericidal activity and less frequent side effects
No difference in early bactericidal activity
items.explanation: The passage states that sutezolid has higher early bactericidal activity compared to linezolid and appears to have less frequent side effects.

items.question: Which enzyme's overexpression can confer resistance to cycloserine in Mycobacterium smegmatis?
items.explanation: Overexpression of alanine racemase can confer resistance to cycloserine in Mycobacterium smegmatis.

items.question: What is a serious potential side effect of cycloserine?
items.explanation: Serious potential side effects of cycloserine include seizures and psychosis.

items.question: What is the usual adult dosage for delamanid?
options: 250 mg two or three times per day
items.explanation: The usual adult dose of delamanid is 100 mg twice daily.

items.question: Which combination has been associated with positive outcomes in the treatment of highly resistant TB?
items.explanation: The combination of meropenem and clavulanic acid has been associated with positive outcomes in the treatment of highly resistant TB.

items.question: Which aminoglycoside is recommended for use despite its serious side effect profile due to the increased availability of novel oral agents?
items.explanation: Amikacin is recommended for use despite its serious side effect profile due to the increased availability of novel oral agents.

items.question: What is a common adverse effect shared by both amikacin and streptomycin?
items.explanation: Both amikacin and streptomycin can cause nephrotoxicity as an adverse effect.

items.question: What is the mechanism of action for ethionamide in treating tuberculosis?
options: Inhibition of enoyl-acyl carrier protein reductase
items.explanation: Ethionamide works by inhibiting the enoyl-acyl carrier protein reductase, which is involved in mycolic acid synthesis.

items.question: Which statement best describes the growth characteristics of rapidly growing NTM species?
options: They require prolonged treatment with multiple medications to be identified.
Their growth is indicative of M. tuberculosis infection.
items.explanation: Rapidly growing NTM species subcultures grow within 1 week.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.question: What is required for the diagnosis of NTM pulmonary disease according to the American Thoracic Society and Infectious Diseases Society of America?
options: Significant clinical manifestations or radiographic evidence of progressive disease consistent with NTM infection, along with reproducible sputum culture results or a single positive culture from bronchoscopy.
Presence of NTM in soil and water.
items.explanation: Diagnosis requires clinical manifestations or radiographic evidence, along with reproducible sputum culture results or a single positive bronchoscopy culture.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.question: Which statement is true regarding the treatment of NTM disease?
options: Treatment regimens are short-term and involve a single medication.
Side effects of the regimens employed are uncommon, requiring continuous therapy.
Treatment depends on the NTM species, extent or type of disease, and drug susceptibility test results.
Isolation from blood is sufficient for initiating treatment.
items.explanation: Treatment regimens depend on the NTM species, disease extent/type, and drug susceptibility tests.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended initial treatment regimen for patients with nodular or bronchiectatic MAC lung disease?
options: Clarithromycin, rifampin, and ethambutol given three times per week
Azithromycin, doxycycline, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole three times per week
items.explanation: The initial regimen for nodular or bronchiectatic MAC lung disease includes clarithromycin or azithromycin, rifampin or rifabutin, and ethambutol given three times per week.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of inhaled liposomal amikacin in the treatment of refractory pulmonary MAC infections?
options: It replaces rifampin in all cases
It increases culture conversion rates when added to a standard three-drug regimen
It is used as monotherapy for initial treatment
It eliminates the need for surgical resection
items.explanation: Inhaled liposomal amikacin, when added to a standard three-drug regimen, significantly increases culture conversion rates in refractory pulmonary MAC infections.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is true regarding treatment for disseminated MAC infection in AIDS patients?
options: Treatment may be lifelong in the absence of immune reconstitution
Therapy is recommended only until culture conversion
Rifampin is always used regardless of resistance
items.explanation: For AIDS patients, treatment for disseminated MAC infection may be lifelong in the absence of immune reconstitution.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended duration of therapy for M. kansasii pulmonary disease after culture conversion?
items.explanation: Therapy for M. kansasii pulmonary disease continues for at least 12 months after culture conversion.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.question: Which organism's treatment is often unsuccessful due to drug adverse effects and toxicities, despite using a regimen comprising at least three active drugs?
items.explanation: M. abscessus treatment is often unsuccessful due to drug adverse effects and toxicities.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.question: What gene in M. abscessus subsp. abscessus is responsible for inducible macrolide resistance?
items.explanation: The erm41 gene in M. abscessus subsp. abscessus is responsible for inducible macrolide resistance.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.question: Which of the following drugs should not be administered with clarithromycin due to potential cardiac arrhythmias?
items.explanation: Clarithromycin should not be administered with cisapride due to potential cardiac arrhythmias.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.question: What is the usual daily dosage of azithromycin for treating MAC infection?
items.explanation: The usual daily dosage of azithromycin for treating MAC infection is 250 mg.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.statement: The isolation of streptomycin in 1943 marked the beginning of antibiotic treatment for tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Streptomycin's discovery was pivotal in initiating antibiotic therapy for TB.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.statement: The preferred treatment duration for latent TB infection with isoniazid and rifampin is 6–9 months.
items.explanation: Shorter duration rifamycin-based regimens are now preferred over the longer 6–9 month isoniazid regimen.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.statement: Intermittent twice-weekly therapy for TB in HIV-co-infected individuals is recommended.
items.explanation: Intermittent twice-weekly therapy for TB in HIV-co-infected individuals is not recommended due to low plasma levels of key TB drugs and higher rates of treatment failure or relapse.

items.statement: Baseline assessment of liver function, including serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and total bilirubin levels, is recommended in adults with TB.
items.explanation: Baseline assessment of liver function is recommended to monitor for hepatotoxicity due to TB medications.

items.statement: The TB PRACTECAL trial found that a 6-month BPaLM regimen was noninferior in efficacy compared to the standard-care regimen.
items.explanation: The TB PRACTECAL trial showed that the 6-month BPaLM regimen was noninferior to the standard-care regimen.

items.statement: Kanamycin and capreomycin are prioritized in current MDR-TB treatment regimens.
items.explanation: Several traditional second-line drugs, including kanamycin and capreomycin, are no longer recommended.

items.statement: Isoniazid requires dosage adjustment in patients with renal disease.
items.explanation: Isoniazid does not require dosage adjustment in patients with renal disease.

items.statement: A weekly regimen of isoniazid and rifapentine for 3 months has shown higher treatment completion rates compared to a daily isoniazid regimen for 9 months.
items.explanation: The weekly regimen of isoniazid and rifapentine over 3 months had a higher treatment completion rate than the single-drug regimen.

items.statement: Isoniazid-associated hepatitis is dose-dependent.
items.explanation: Isoniazid-associated hepatitis is idiosyncratic, not dose-dependent.

items.statement: Rifampin decreases the half-life of some drugs by inducing the hepatic cytochrome P450 system.
items.explanation: As a potent inducer of the hepatic cytochrome P450 system, rifampin can decrease the half-life of some drugs.

items.statement: Dose adjustments of rifampin are necessary in patients with renal insufficiency.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that no adjustments of dose or frequency are necessary for rifampin in patients with renal insufficiency.

items.statement: Ethambutol is the most potent first-line drug against M. tuberculosis.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that ethambutol is the least potent among first-line drugs against M. tuberculosis.

items.statement: Ethambutol is bactericidal against M. tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Ethambutol is bacteriostatic, not bactericidal.

items.statement: Mutations at codon 306 of the embB gene are associated with ethambutol resistance.
items.explanation: Mutations at codon 306 in the embB gene are linked to increased MICs and clinical resistance.

items.statement: Rifapentine is recommended as a first-line treatment option for children under 12 years of age with TB.
items.explanation: Rifapentine has not been approved for use in children <12 years of age.

items.statement: Mutations that cause resistance to rifampin also cause resistance to rifapentine.
items.explanation: Rifapentine resistance is mediated by mutations in rpoB, similar to rifampin.

items.statement: The absorption of fluoroquinolones is increased by co-ingestion with products containing multivalent cations.
items.explanation: The absorption of fluoroquinolones is decreased by co-ingestion with products containing multivalent cations.

items.statement: Bedaquiline resistance has been reported due to point mutations in the atpE gene.
items.explanation: Resistance to bedaquiline is associated with point mutations in the atpE gene.

items.statement: Bedaquiline is recommended for use in all shorter course, oral MDR-TB treatment regimens by the WHO.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that bedaquiline is an integral part of all shorter course, oral MDR treatment regimens endorsed by the WHO.

items.statement: Linezolid's mechanism of action involves inhibition of DNA gyrase.
items.explanation: The passage states that linezolid disrupts protein synthesis by binding to the 50S bacterial ribosome, not by inhibiting DNA gyrase.

items.statement: Cycloserine is recommended for use in children younger than 6 years with rifampicin-resistant TB.
items.explanation: Delamanid, not cycloserine, is recommended for use in children younger than 6 years with rifampicin-resistant TB.

items.statement: Pretomanid has been approved by the FDA for specific highly resistant TB cases.
items.explanation: The FDA has granted approval for pretomanid in specific highly resistant TB cases based on results from the BPaL regimen study.

items.statement: Kanamycin and capreomycin are recommended for treating tuberculosis due to their efficacy.
items.explanation: Kanamycin and capreomycin are no longer recommended due to worse treatment outcomes and increased mortality.

items.statement: Streptomycin is bactericidal against non-dividing M. tuberculosis organisms.
items.explanation: Streptomycin is bactericidal against dividing M. tuberculosis organisms, not non-dividing ones.

items.statement: Isolation of NTM from blood or an infected extrapulmonary site is usually indicative of disseminated or local NTM infection.
items.explanation: Isolation from these sites indicates disseminated or local NTM infection.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.statement: The majority of the more than 180 species of NTM are important human pathogens.
items.explanation: Only a minority of the over 180 NTM species are important human pathogens.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.statement: Routine initial testing for macrolide resistance is recommended in the treatment of MAC lung disease.
items.explanation: Routine initial testing for macrolide resistance is indeed recommended to guide therapy.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.statement: Drug susceptibility testing for M. marinum should be performed routinely.
items.explanation: Drug susceptibility testing for M. marinum is recommended only if the swab remains culture positive after 3 months of appropriate therapy.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.statement: Clarithromycin levels are decreased by rifampin.
items.explanation: Rifampin lowers clarithromycin levels.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.statement: Azithromycin requires dosage adjustment based on renal function.
items.explanation: Adjustment of the dosage of azithromycin is not necessary based on renal function.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.assertion: The treatment of latent TB infection in HIV-positive individuals requires special consideration.
items.reason: There is a potential for drug interactions and lack of definitive data on effectiveness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why caution is advised.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.assertion: The traditional treatment regimen for drug-susceptible TB disease includes a continuation phase of 6 months.
items.reason: This regimen consists of isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for the intensive phase.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain why the continuation phase lasts 6 months.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · Antimycobacterial Agents | GENERAL PRINCIPLES (part 1)

items.assertion: TB immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) can manifest as a paradoxical worsening of TB infection.
items.reason: This occurs due to the initiation of antiretroviral therapy in HIV patients co-infected with M. tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the manifestation of IRIS.

items.assertion: Multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB) is defined as resistance to both isoniazid and rifampin.
items.reason: The risk of MDR-TB is elevated in patients from geographic areas with ≥5% incident cases being MDR-TB.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why MDR-TB is defined as resistance to both isoniazid and rifampin.

items.assertion: Isoniazid is bacteriostatic against slowly dividing organisms.
items.reason: Isoniazid has excellent bactericidal activity against actively dividing M. tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.

items.assertion: Isoniazid is metabolized in the liver via acetylation by NAT2.
items.reason: Acetylation affects serum levels and potential toxicity of isoniazid.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Isoniazid is associated with peripheral neuropathy.
items.reason: Isoniazid interferes with pyridoxine (vitamin B₆) metabolism, increasing the risk of neurotoxicity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Rifampin is ineffective against nondividing M. tuberculosis.
items.reason: Rifampin has both sterilizing and bactericidal activity against dividing and nondividing M. tuberculosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; rifampin is effective against both dividing and nondividing M. tuberculosis.

items.assertion: Pyrazinamide's antimycobacterial activity is limited to M. tuberculosis.
items.reason: Pyrazinamide is converted by mycobacterial pyrimidase to the active form, pyrazinoic acid, which acts in acidic environments.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason provided does not explain why its activity is limited to M. tuberculosis.

items.assertion: Hepatotoxicity due to rifampin alone is common.
items.reason: Adverse events associated with rifampin are infrequent and generally mild, often consisting of isolated hyperbilirubinemia rather than aminotransferase elevation.
items.explanation: The assertion that hepatotoxicity due to rifampin alone is common is false; the passage states it is uncommon.

items.assertion: Rifabutin is more active in vitro than rifampin against MAC organisms and other NTM.
items.reason: Rifabutin's clinical superiority over rifampin has been established for these infections.
items.explanation: While rifabutin is more active in vitro, its clinical superiority has not been established.

items.assertion: Rifapentine's absorption is improved when taken with food.
items.reason: Food intake enhances the bioavailability of rifapentine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Fluoroquinolones are considered safe alternatives for patients who develop treatment-limiting adverse effects from first-line agents.
items.reason: Fluoroquinolones have relatively infrequent adverse effects and are well tolerated during TB treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why fluoroquinolones are considered safe alternatives.

items.assertion: Multiple courses of empirical fluoroquinolone therapy for presumed community-acquired pneumonia can lead to delayed diagnosis of active pulmonary TB.
items.reason: Mutations in the genes encoding DNA gyrase are implicated in all cases of clinical resistance to fluoroquinolones.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because mutations in DNA gyrase genes do not account for all cases of resistance.

items.assertion: Clofazimine can promote killing of antibiotic-tolerant M. tuberculosis persister organisms.
items.reason: By increasing reactive oxidant species and causing membrane destabilization, clofazimine may enhance bacterial cell death.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the mechanism by which clofazimine promotes killing of persister organisms.

items.assertion: Linezolid is typically administered at a dose of 600 mg twice daily for MDR-TB treatment.
items.reason: A single daily dose of linezolid is associated with fewer adverse events than twice-a-day dosing.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the passage states that linezolid is usually administered at a dose of 600 mg once daily for MDR-TB treatment, not twice daily.

items.assertion: Cycloserine should be administered with caution to patients with epilepsy.
items.reason: Cycloserine can cause seizures and psychosis, which are serious side effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the risk of seizures justifies cautious use in epileptic patients.

items.assertion: Carbapenems are widely used for TB treatment due to their oral availability.
items.reason: Carbapenems need to be administered intravenously and have unclear long-term side effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because carbapenems are not widely used for TB treatment due to the lack of oral availability.

items.assertion: The growth characteristics of NTM have diagnostic, therapeutic, and prognostic implications.
items.reason: Rapidly growing species subcultures grow within 1 week, providing useful preliminary information in a clinical context.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why growth characteristics have diagnostic implications.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · DRUGS IN DEVELOPMENT | NONTUBERCULOUS MYCOBACTERIA

items.assertion: Surgical resection is considered for MAC infections localized to one lung.
items.reason: It should be considered when there's a poor response to medical therapy and adequate lung function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why surgical resection may be considered.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.assertion: Inhaled liposomal amikacin is approved as a first-line agent for MAC lung disease.
items.reason: It has been shown to increase culture conversion rates in treatment-refractory cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because it is currently being evaluated as a first-line agent, not yet approved. The reason is true.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 1)

items.assertion: Macrolide resistance in M. abscessus is associated with worse outcomes.
items.reason: M. abscessus isolates are usually resistant to standard anti-TB regimens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the association of macrolide resistance with worse outcomes.
items.reference: Antimycobacterial Agents · THERAPEUTIC CONSIDERATIONS FOR SPECIFIC NTM (part 2) | DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM

items.subtopic: DRUGS FOR THE TREATMENT OF NTM
items.assertion: Azithromycin is preferred over clarithromycin due to its better tolerability and fewer drug interactions.
items.reason: Azithromycin has a prolonged half-life of approximately 48 hours.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why azithromycin is preferred over clarithromycin.
